title: Board and Batten Media Console
summary: A media console with two drawers and two board-and-batten doors on 2x2 legs. Cottage styling over honest 1x12 casework.
description:
This console dresses up simple 1x12 casework with a board-and-batten front — the vertical batten strips on the doors and the chunky 2x2 legs and trim give it a cottage weight that plain plywood wouldn't. Underneath it's approachable: a 1x12 box, two drawers, and two doors, joined with pocket holes or countersunk screws.

The framework matters more than any single joint. The side trim, the legs, and the front/back 2x2 trim each add a layer of rigidity, so even though the box is nailable, the finished piece ends up genuinely stout. Attach the legs flush to the inside of the box and the outside of the side trim; fasten the front/back trim the same way and tie the legs to the trim — that's what stiffens it.

The drawers are lifted straight from the site's nightstand plans (intentionally identical), and the same rule applies: measure the exact finished opening and build the drawers and guides to it, shimming the bottom guides ~1/8" for a perfect-fitting drawer. Cut the door and drawer face parts to your real openings, not the drawing.

tools.note: Or countersunk screws / nails with lots of glue.

materials.name: 2x2, 8 ft
materials.note: Legs, front/back trim, drawer/door trim, drawer guides.

materials.name: 1x2, 8 ft
materials.note: Side trim, door battens, drawer guides.

materials.name: 1/4" plywood
materials.note: Door backs and optional console back.

cutList.note: Cut to the width of your 1x12.
Cut to the width of your 1x12.

steps.title: Cut all the parts
steps.body: Cut two sides at 19-1/2", one top at 57-1/2", two center partitions at 18-3/4", one box bottom at 54-1/2" and two drawer bottoms at 18-3/4" from the 1x12. Cut four side trim at 11-1/2", one top trim back at 57-1/2" and six door battens / stiles at 11-3/8" from the 1x2. Cut four legs at 23-1/4", four front/back trim at 54-1/2" and four drawer/door trim at 16-1/2" from the 2x2. Cut one top trim front at 57-1/2" and four door rails at 15-3/8" from the 1x3. Cut four drawer front/back at 20-1/4" and four drawer sides at 11-1/2" from the 1x8. Cut two door backs at 16-1/4" from the 1/4" plywood. Allowing about 1/8" for each saw cut, that comes to three 8 ft 1x12, two 8 ft 1x2, four 8 ft 2x2, two 8 ft 1x3 and one 12 ft 1x8. Square every cut and label each piece as it comes off the saw — several parts here are within half an inch of each other and are easy to mix up later.

steps.title: Attach the sides to the side trim
steps.body: Attach each side to its side trim as shown — do two. Fastening from the inside hides the screw holes. This is the first of several layers that stiffen the finished piece.

steps.title: Build the box
steps.body: Assemble the two 19-1/2" sides, the 57-1/2" top, the 54-1/2" bottom and the two 18-3/4" partitions into a box: the bottom runs between the sides, so the carcass finishes 56" wide over its outside faces. Set the two partitions to divide the interior into the two door bays and the center drawer bay. Pocket screws sized for 3/4" stock are the cleanest joint here — note the materials list only carries 2-1/2" screws, which will blow straight through 3/4" pine, so buy short pocket screws or fall back to countersunk screws and glue. Check both diagonals before the glue grabs; every door and drawer downstream is fighting for half an inch and a racked box takes that half inch away.

steps.title: Add the legs and front/back trim
steps.body: Attach the four 23-1/4" legs flush to the inside of the box and the outside of the side trim, so each leg projects 3/4" past the 56" carcass — that puts the width across the legs at 57-1/2", which is the length of the top, so the top finishes flush at the ends rather than overhanging. Then run the four 54-1/2" 2x2 front and back trims between the legs, two at the front and two at the back, and tie the legs into the trim with glue and screws or pocket holes from inside. That 54-1/2" between the legs is the whole front of the console, and everything on the face has to fit inside it.

steps.title: Finish the top trim
steps.body: Glue and pin the 57-1/2" 1x2 to the back edge of the top and the 57-1/2" 1x3 to the front edge; both run the full length of the top, matching it end to end. If you are using pocket holes, drill them in the underside of the 1x12 top and pull the trim up from below so nothing shows on the finished surface. Keep the front trim's top face dead level with the top — a proud edge on a media console catches every remote and cable that slides across it.

steps.title: Build the drawers and guides — and check the face first
steps.body: Before cutting a drawer part, measure the finished face: it is 54-1/2" between the legs, and the two 16-1/2" 2x2 drawer/door trims that split it into three bays take 3" of that, leaving 51-1/2" of actual opening. The cut list does not fit inside it — two 15-3/8" doors plus a box built from the 20-1/4" front and back pieces total 51", and the doors' own 1/8" reveals eat the remaining 1/2" before any guide is added, so the drawer as drawn cannot be installed. Divide your real 51-1/2" among the three bays first, then cut the drawer front and back to the center bay less the guides you intend to use (3/4" per side off a 1x2, 1-1/2" off a 2x2) and the 11-1/2" sides to your case depth. Set the bottom guides first, shimming up about 1/8" until the drawer runs level, then add the side guides; also note the 1x2 has only about 20" of offcut left across its two boards, so plan the guide stock before you cut.

steps.title: Build the doors, then finish
steps.body: Each door is two 1x3 rails top and bottom, three 1x2 battens between them, and a 1/4" plywood back — the cut list draws them at 15-3/8" wide by 16-1/4" tall, but cut them to whatever the two outer bays actually measured in the last step, less 1/4" in each direction for an even 1/8" reveal all round. Glue and brad the battens to the plywood back, then pin the rails on, keeping the frame square so the door does not sit twisted in its opening. Hang the doors, fit the four knobs or handles across the doors and drawers, then fill, sand and finish — the board-and-batten face takes paint and primer beautifully, and paint also hides the plywood door backs' edge grain.

images.alt: A media console with two drawers and two board-and-batten doors on chunky legs
